comments:
Summary
This paper proposes “half-sibling regression”, a simple procedure to remove systematic confounding noise from an observed signal Y by regressing Y on a set of other observables X that share the same noise source N, and using the regression residual as an estimator of the latent signal Q. The authors give a clear causal motivation, provide theoretical results (Proposition 1–5 and supporting lemmas) quantifying identifiability and error under various conditions (complete/incomplete information, many predictors, small additive independent noise, time-series extensions), and demonstrate the method on synthetic data and a challenging real application: removing Kepler spacecraft systematics from stellar light curves. They compare to the Kepler PDC pipeline and report improved CDPP (12-hour) on a large sample of stars. Code is released.
Strengths
- Clear, principled idea with an intuitive causal interpretation: regress out components of Y predictable from X and treat residuals as denoised signal. The paper carefully states the assumptions (additive model Y = Q + f(N) and X ⊥ Q) and the unavoidable gauge freedom (identifiability up to invertible reparametrization/offset).
- Theoretical analysis: Propositions and lemmas provide useful bounds and consistency statements in reasonable regimes (many predictors, small independent noise in predictors). Proofs are short and convincing in the provided text.
- Strong, relevant application: Kepler light-curve de-trending is a real, high-impact problem. The empirical work shows practical choices (linear models with L2 regularization, cross-validation, autoregressive inputs with an exclusion window) and claims substantial improvements versus PDC in CDPP.
- Practical considerations are discussed (risk of removing intrinsic variability, time-series confounding, choice of predictors, multiplicative vs additive effects and calibration), and code is made available.
Weaknesses and concerns
- Strong model assumptions: the basic identifiability statements rely on the additive form Y = Q + f(N) and on X being independent of Q. These conditions are reasonable in many instrument-noise settings but not universally applicable. The paper acknowledges this, but more extensive diagnostics or robustness checks would strengthen practical claims.
- Risk of removing true signal: the authors note that if predictor stars share intrinsic variability with the target, the regression can attenuate Q. While the AR/exclusion-window mitigation is sensible, a more detailed empirical study of the rate at which real transits or other astrophysical signals could be attenuated (or an analysis of false negative risk) is desirable.
- Empirical details: the reported empirical advantages (CDPP improvements over PDC) are compelling but the excerpt lacks detailed quantitative results (summary statistics, distributions, statistical significance) and ablation studies: sensitivity to number of predictor pixels, regularization strength, predictor selection strategy, different magnitudes and crowding conditions, runtime and scalability on many targets. The paper references figures but more tabled numbers and error bars would help readers assess robustness.
- Time series treatment: the paper discusses temporal confounding and proposes including lagged predictors and an exclusion window; theoretical treatment of the time-series case is informal and empirical evaluation of different window sizes and their effect on transit recovery would be useful.
- Comparison baselines: PDC is an appropriate and strong baseline, but additional comparisons (e.g., other common de-trending or factor-analysis approaches, and results with nonlinear regressors) would strengthen confidence that the method’s simplicity is sufficient across regimes.
Suggestions for improvement
- Include more quantitative empirical results: tables summarizing CDPP improvements across magnitude bins, distributions, significance tests, and results from ablation studies (varying predictor count, regularization, AR window size).
- Provide experiments that quantify the probability of attenuating true transits (e.g., by injecting synthetic transits of varying depth and duration into light curves before de-trending and measuring recovery rates).
- Add diagnostic methods to detect violations of X ⊥ Q in practice (e.g., tests for predictor-target correlation structure that may indicate removal of astrophysical variability) and guidelines for predictor selection to avoid accidental signal leakage.
- Expand the time-series analysis: either theoretical identifiability results accounting for temporal dependence or more systematic empirical evaluation of lagged predictors and exclusion windows.
- Clarify scalability and runtime for processing many targets and discuss how hyperparameters were set in large-scale runs.
Conclusion
This is a well-written, well-motivated paper that combines a simple, interpretable method, supporting theory, and a convincing real-world application with promising empirical gains. The assumptions and limitations are acknowledged, but more empirical diagnostics and sensitivity analyses would increase confidence in widespread adoption. I recommend acceptance and an oral presentation so the community can assess and build on this practical contribution to de-trending systematic errors in observational data.
